message.content:
Implementation files: src/game-of-life.ts
Test files: src/game-of-life.spec.ts
Passing tests: 11

Run the final metric-driven refactoring pass over the whole src/.
Iterate ONE change at a time with pre/post measurement (ESLint, cognitive,
APP, McCabe). Stop when no metric improves further or no improvement is
possible. Return after completion.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: Now let me run ESLint to get the baseline PRE measurements.

input.command: pnpm exec eslint src/ --format json 2>&1 | tail -200

content.text:
## Iteration 1 — PRE Measurements

**ESLint smells**: 2 (both `no-magic-numbers` on line 12)

**APP Mass per function**:

| Function | Constants | Bindings | Invocations | Conditionals | Loops | Assignments | APP Mass |
|----------|-----------|----------|-------------|--------------|-------|-------------|----------|
| `keyOf` | 0 | 2 (x,y) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 2 |
| `isAliveNextGen` | 2 (3, 2) | 2 (isLive, count) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 4 |
| `neighborsOf` | 0 | 4 (x,y,dx,dy) | 1 (map) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 6 |
| `tallyNeighborCounts` | 1 (0) | 4 (tallies, neighbor, key, tally) | 4 (Map, flatMap, get, set, keyOf) | 0 | 1 (for) | 2 (set, tallies init) | ~21 |
| `nextGeneration` | 0 | 2 (liveCells, liveKeys) | 4 (Set, map, keyOf, tally..., filter, map, isAliveNextGen, has) | 0 | 0 | 0 | ~14 |

**McCabe Cyclomatic Complexity**:

| Function | McCabe |
|----------|--------|
| `keyOf` | 1 |
| `isAliveNextGen` | 3 (||, &&) |
| `neighborsOf` | 1 |
| `tallyNeighborCounts` | 2 (for + ??) |
| `nextGeneration` | 1 |

**Naming Evaluation**: All names are clean and reveal intent given the full test suite (`keyOf`, `isAliveNextGen`, `neighborsOf`, `tallyNeighborCounts`, `nextGeneration`).

**Worst offender**: `tallyNeighborCounts` has highest APP mass and a smell at the magic numbers, but the smells (`no-magic-numbers`) are in `isAliveNextGen`. Let me fix the most concrete issue first — the 2 magic-number smells.

## Iteration 1 — Apply Refactoring

Extract the magic numbers `3` (reproduction count) and `2` (survival count) as named constants.

input.old_string:
const isAliveNextGen = (isLive: boolean, neighborCount: number): boolean =>
  neighborCount === 3 || (isLive && neighborCount === 2);

content.text:
I'll continue with the metric-driven refactor pass; task tracking isn't needed for this short iteration loop.

## Iteration 1 — POST Measurements & Delta

- **ESLint smells (whole src/)**: 2 → 0 (removed both `no-magic-numbers`)
- **APP mass `isAliveNextGen`**: 4 → 6 (added 2 binding references, removed 2 constants — net +2 because the constant declarations are added at module scope, but reading clarity is significantly better)
- **McCabe `isAliveNextGen`**: 3 → 3 (unchanged)
- **Tests**: 11/11 passing

Net: smells dropped to 0; APP slightly up but clarity (Rule 2) wins. Continue.

## Iteration 2 — PRE Measurements

ESLint smells: 0. No more concrete rule violations.

Let me look at the worst remaining APP mass — `tallyNeighborCounts`:

```typescript
const tallyNeighborCounts = (liveCells: Cell[]): Map<string, Tally> => {
  const tallies = new Map<string, Tally>();
  for (const neighbor of liveCells.flatMap(neighborsOf)) {
    const key = keyOf(neighbor);
    const tally = tallies.get(key) ?? { cell: neighbor, count: 0 };
    tallies.set(key, { ...tally, count: tally.count + 1 });
  }
  return tallies;
};
```

This function has assignment operations (Map mutation) and a `for` loop. McCabe = 2.

Also, looking at `nextGeneration`, the destructuring `[...tallyNeighborCounts(liveCells)]` followed by `.filter(...).map(...)` reads the Map values via tuple destructure. This is OK but there's some indirection — we could iterate just over the values instead of entries since the cell is stored in the tally.

Let me consider: the `key` is only used to lookup `liveKeys.has(key)`. Since the tally also has the cell, we can store live status directly in the tally or filter using cell coords. Actually, a cleaner refactor: pass `liveKeys` into the tally function so we don't need keys at all in `nextGeneration`.

Better: simplify by working over values not entries.

Let me try: have `tallyNeighborCounts` return just an iterable of `{cell, count}` and have `nextGeneration` recompute the key inline. But this re-computes keys.

Alternative: rewrite `nextGeneration` to iterate Map values:

```typescript
return [...tallyNeighborCounts(liveCells).entries()]
  .filter(([key, { count }]) => isAliveNextGen(liveKeys.has(key), count))
  .map(([, { cell }]) => cell);
```

vs.

```typescript
return [...tallyNeighborCounts(liveCells).values()]
  .filter(({ cell, count }) => isAliveNextGen(liveKeys.has(keyOf(cell)), count))
  .map(({ cell }) => cell);
```

Second has more invocations (keyOf called per entry). Worse APP.

What if we change Tally to also store `isLive`? Then we can avoid `liveKeys` entirely outside. But that requires passing `liveKeys` into the tally function. Let me think about whether a single-pass approach is cleaner.

Actually, looking more carefully, there's an interesting simplification opportunity. The current approach:
1. Build `liveKeys` Set
2. Build `tallies` Map of neighbor counts
3. Filter and map

We could merge steps by including the cell's own "self count" as part of the tally — but the rule uses `neighborCount` (excluding self).

Let me try a different refactor — extract a `Tally` builder that includes `isLive` so `nextGeneration` becomes one filter+map without the `liveKeys.has` lookup:

Let me think about the Tally type — currently it doesn't carry the `isLive` flag. The redundant key bookkeeping in `nextGeneration` is the main inefficiency. Let me check what happens if `Tally` carries `isLive`:

Try: include `isLive` in tally so caller doesn't need the Set lookup.

content.text:
## Iteration 2 — POST Measurements & Delta

- **ESLint smells (whole src/)**: 0 → 0
- **APP mass `nextGeneration`**: ~14 → ~8 (removed `liveKeys` binding+map+keyOf+spread; just `.filter().map()` over the tallies)
- **APP mass `tallyNeighborCounts`**: ~21 → ~22 (added liveKeys init inside)
- **Net APP mass**: ~35 → ~30 (improvement)
- **McCabe `tallyNeighborCounts`**: 2 → 2
- **McCabe `nextGeneration`**: 1 → 1
- **Tests**: 11/11 passing

Improvement: simpler `nextGeneration` (no Set/keyOf duplication at the call site), `tallyNeighborCounts` now returns the natural shape for downstream use.

## Iteration 3 — PRE Measurements

**Naming Review**: 
- `tallyNeighborCounts` — returns Tally[] with cell+count+isLive. The name now slightly understates what it returns (it tags isLive too). Maybe `tallyCandidates` or `surveyNeighborhood`? Let me hold this thought.
- `isAliveNextGen` — clear.
- `neighborsOf` — clear.

**APP mass current state**:

| Function | APP Mass | McCabe |
|----------|----------|--------|
| `keyOf` | 2 | 1 |
| `isAliveNextGen` | 6 | 3 |
| `neighborsOf` | 6 | 1 |
| `tallyNeighborCounts` | ~22 | 2 |
| `nextGeneration` | ~8 | 1 |

`tallyNeighborCounts` is still the heaviest. The for-loop with `tallies.get/set` + spread is the heaviest part. The spread `{ ...tally, count: tally.count + 1 }` creates a new object every iteration, which doubles allocations. We could mutate the Tally directly since it's owned within this function.

Let me try: mutate the count in place to reduce assignments and spreads.

content.text:
## Iteration 3 — POST Measurements & Delta

Wait — but I introduced an `if/else` and an explicit mutation. McCabe increased from 2 to 3, and the mutation `existing.count += 1` adds assignment cost. Let me check the trade:

- **ESLint smells**: 0 → 0
- **APP mass `tallyNeighborCounts`**: ~22 → ~24 (added if/else conditional +4, removed spread+`{ ...tally, count: tally.count + 1 }`, added explicit assignment)
- **McCabe `tallyNeighborCounts`**: 2 → 3 (added if-else branch)

This change made McCabe WORSE. Even though it's arguably more performant, the metrics got worse without a smell-reducing payoff. **Revert** and stick with the prior cleaner version.

input.description: Confirm tests still pass after revert

content.content:
Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  11 passed (11)
   Start at  14:24:36
   Duration  152ms (transform 24ms, setup 0ms, collect 23ms, tests 4ms, environment 0ms, prepare 42ms)

content.text:
Revert confirmed. Now let me try another angle.

## Iteration 3' — Alternative

Looking at `tallyNeighborCounts`, the inner double-lookup (`get` + `set`) is the main cost-driver. Alternative: use a reduce over a single flatMap of neighbors. Doesn't reduce APP much.

Another alternative: split `tallyNeighborCounts` purpose so it only counts; build `isLive` in `nextGeneration`. But that's reverting Iteration 2.

Let me consider whether `keyOf` is being computed redundantly. In `tallyNeighborCounts`, `liveKeys.has(key)` could be checked using the cell directly via a Set of stringified cells. Already is. The `keyOf(neighbor)` is called per neighbor. The construction of `liveKeys` uses keyOf again. These are necessary for correctness.

Let me look at extracting the inner update logic to a named helper. Actually, with current state, there's no clear win remaining without trading clarity.

Let me check if extracting the neighbor iteration as a clearer reduce would help:

## Iteration 3 (new try) — Try a different angle

Look at `nextGeneration` filter callback — it does `({ isLive, count }) => isAliveNextGen(isLive, count)`. This adapter is just unpacking and forwarding. Can we make `isAliveNextGen` take a `Tally` directly?

Wait, but isAliveNextGen is a pure rule predicate. Making it take Tally would couple the rule to internal data structure. Rule 2 (reveals intent) prefers a clean predicate signature.

Alternative: rename `isAliveNextGen` to `survivesOrIsBorn` to better signal intent? Let me check naming.

Actually `isAliveNextGen` literally describes "is alive next gen given current state and neighbor count" — clear and correct.

Let me look for further removals. The Map intermediate could potentially be eliminated with a different algorithm structure, but it's the natural data structure for the tally pattern.

Let me check naming once more across the file:
- `keyOf` — clear
- `isAliveNextGen` — clear; could also be `willBeAliveNext`. "isAlive" suggests state; "willBe" suggests transition. Marginal — keep.
- `neighborsOf` — clear
- `tallyNeighborCounts` — now returns enriched tallies, not just counts. Maybe `surveyNeighborhood` or `tallyCells`? Renaming this is mostly cosmetic; current name is acceptable.

Let me try one more measurement angle — can the `liveKeys` Set be replaced with an inline lookup against `liveCells.flatMap(...)` source? No — that would be O(n²). The Set is necessary.

What about the line `for (const neighbor of liveCells.flatMap(neighborsOf))` — creating intermediate array via `flatMap`. Could use nested for loops to avoid allocation, but that increases McCabe by adding another loop +5 mass.

I think the code is at a stable minimum. Let me confirm by trying one more candidate: extracting the new-tally creation as a helper.

Actually one possible refactor: the line `tallies.set(key, { ...tally, count: tally.count + 1 });` has spread + binding access. A cleaner pattern using `Map.set` with a fluent style won't reduce mass.

Let me verify final state.

## Iteration 3 — Conclude: No improvement possible

After trying mutation-based update (Iteration 3, reverted) and considering several other angles, no further measured improvement is achievable without sacrificing clarity (Rule 2 trumps APP).

**Final whole-src state**:

content.text:
End-Refactor Complete.

Iterations applied: 2 productive iterations + 1 reverted attempt.

Per-iteration summary:

**Iteration 1** — Extracted magic numbers `3` and `2` as named constants `REPRODUCTION_NEIGHBORS` and `SURVIVAL_NEIGHBORS` in `src/game-of-life.ts`:
- ESLint smells: 2 -> 0 (removed both `no-magic-numbers`)
- Cognitive: unchanged (all functions already trivial)
- APP mass `isAliveNextGen`: 4 -> 6 (named bindings cost more than literals; clarity win, Rule 2 trumps APP)
- McCabe `isAliveNextGen`: 3 -> 3 (unchanged)

**Iteration 2** — Pushed `isLive` lookup into `tallyNeighborCounts` so `nextGeneration` no longer needs its own `Set` + `keyOf`:
- ESLint smells: 0 -> 0
- APP mass `nextGeneration`: ~14 -> ~8 (removed liveKeys Set construction at call site)
- APP mass `tallyNeighborCounts`: ~21 -> ~22 (absorbed the liveKeys init)
- Net APP across both: ~35 -> ~30
- McCabe `tallyNeighborCounts`: 2 -> 2 (unchanged)
- McCabe `nextGeneration`: 1 -> 1 (unchanged)

**Iteration 3 (attempted, reverted)** — Tried replacing `tallies.get(k) ?? newTally` + spread-set with an `if/else` mutating `existing.count`:
- McCabe `tallyNeighborCounts`: 2 -> 3 (worse — added a branch)
- No smell payoff
- Reverted; current `?? + spread` form keeps the function at McCabe 2 with no conditional.

After Iteration 3's revert, every additional angle I considered (nested loops to avoid `flatMap`; pushing Tally into `isAliveNextGen` to remove the unpacker; renaming `tallyNeighborCounts`) either traded a metric worse or only moved cost between functions without net improvement.

Whole-src final state:
- Total ESLint smells: 0
- Max cognitive complexity: trivial across all functions (no SonarJS report fires)
- Max APP mass: ~22 (in `tallyNeighborCounts`, justified by being the core algorithm)
- Max McCabe cyclomatic: 3 (in `isAliveNextGen` — `||` + `&&` — inherent to the survival/reproduction rule)
- All function names accurately describe their purpose given the full test suite.

Tests: 11/11 passing.

Relevant files:
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_13-59-54_game-of-life-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.ts`
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_13-59-54_game-of-life-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.spec.ts`
